Locked In.
UX Case Study Business Case Study ↗ ← Portfolio
UX Case Study · Talent Operations · 2025–2026

HOW IT
GOT BUILT.

Four versions. One insight that changed everything. The decisions, the mistakes, and the actual screenshots — from a Google Sheet with a custom menu to a production React app.

V0 → V1 → V2 → V3 React ~14 months 4 major versions
Version 0
The Spreadsheet
Early 2025 · Google Sheets + Apps Script

The first version wasn't a product — it was a hypothesis. A Google Sheet with a custom "Interview Scheduler" menu powered by Apps Script. The coordinator picked an interviewer, set a week and duration, then manually triggered scripts row by row to find calendar slots and generate invite links.

There was no candidate flow. No submission model. No UI. Just a spreadsheet with a custom menu and a lot of manual steps between every action.

V0: The spreadsheet — Interviewer, Calendar ID, Week of, Status, Duration, Suggested Slots. The "Interview Scheduler" custom menu visible at top — everything triggered manually per row.
💡
What it proved: Calendar integration was the right anchor. Slot-finding and duration awareness worked. But one row = one interview, no candidate data, no recruiter flow. The coordinator did everything themselves. That had to change.
Version 1
Booked
Mid 2025 · Vanilla HTML/CSS/JS · Single flow

The first real UI. A 4-step wizard: Candidate → Interview → Availability → Confirm. Dashboard and Reports tabs. The step structure, card design, and visual language from this version all carried forward into Locked In.

But one structural decision baked in from day one would cause problems only visible after real user testing: one linear flow for everyone. Recruiters and coordinators shared the same screen, and neither had a flow designed for how they actually work.

V1 Booked: Step 1 of 4 — Schedule / Dashboard / Report tabs at top. Step bar: Candidate → Interview → Availability → Confirm. One flow, one user type assumed.
✗
The fatal flaw: Step 2 asked the recruiter to configure interview stage, duration, and mode — the coordinator's decisions. Step 3 used date/time pickers for availability, but candidates write in natural language: "I'm free Monday morning or Thursday after 2." Every step carried the wrong mental model for at least one of the two people using it.
✓
What it got right: The card + step structure, visual language, dashboard pattern, and email confirmation preview — all carried forward into Locked In.
The Turning Point

THE INSIGHT THAT
CHANGED EVERYTHING.

After a UX review with a coordinator and researcher, the structural problem became undeniable. Recruiters and coordinators have fundamentally different jobs in the scheduling workflow. One submits. One executes. They should never share the same screen.

"A tool built for everyone is built for no one. The moment we split the personas, every screen got simpler — because it finally had a single job."

Before: Booked — single flow
✗Recruiter set interview stage, duration, mode — coordinator's decisions
✗Date/time pickers for availability — wrong format entirely
✗No queue — submissions had nowhere to land
✗One interview per submission — loops needed multiple forms
✗No auth, no persistence — data lost on refresh
After: Locked In. — split personas
✓Recruiter: 3 steps — candidate info, paste availability, submit. Done.
✓Free-text availability — AI parses it into time slots
✓Jira-style ticket queue — submissions land, coordinator picks up
✓Multi-interview builder — full loops in a single request
✓Firebase backend — real auth, real-time sync, persistent data
Version 2
Locked In.
Early 2026 · Persona split · Ticket queue · Firebase

The rebrand wasn't cosmetic. "Booked" said nothing. "Locked In." is what a recruiter says when an interview is confirmed — it's the moment the work is done. The name change signaled the tool had found its identity.

The structural changes were the real story: a toggle splits the app into two distinct experiences connected by a shared ticket queue.

Locked In. login screen — Google Sign-In, team isolation by email domain. The first thing that signals this is a different product.
The Coordinator View

THE QUEUE IS THE
CENTRAL DESIGN DECISION.

Coordinators don't think in calendar blocks. They think in requests. "I have 4 things to get through today." The ticket queue — with Pending and Scheduled tabs, status badges, resume links, slot counts — maps directly to how coordinators actually talk about their work.

Clicking a ticket opens the full detail: candidate info, raw availability text, slot picker per interview, dual calendar previews, and a single button that confirms the schedule and opens Google Calendar with everything pre-filled.

Scheduling Queue — Pending tab. Each ticket shows candidate, role, recruiter, interview count, slot count, resume link, and timestamp.
Scheduling Queue — Scheduled tab. Green status badges. The queue becomes a record of everything that's been handled.
🔑
Key UX principle: The recruiter's job ends at Submit. The coordinator's job starts at the queue. Neither person has to think about the other's workflow. The queue is the handoff point — where two experiences connect without overlapping.

The coordinator view doesn't stop at the queue. Dashboard shows upcoming scheduled interviews by date. Reports let you filter by period, department, recruiter, or role. Historic archives past interviews automatically.

Interview Dashboard — upcoming scheduled interviews shown by date with mode chips (Virtual/Phone/Onsite), interview name, recruiter, and confirmed time slot.
Reports — By Role view, All Roles. 20 total interviews, 2 departments, 4 recruiters. Bar chart breakdown by role.
Reports filtered to a single role — Senior Marketing Manager. The dropdown filter lets coordinators slice data without rebuilding the report.
Historic Interviews — searchable archive of past interviews. Auto-archived when scheduled date passes. Search by candidate, role, or recruiter.
Version 3
React + Firebase
March 2026 · Production · Real-time · Multi-user

The vanilla JS version proved the concept. The React rebuild turned it into something you could hand to a team. The codebase moved from a 2,000-line single file to a proper component architecture — each view its own file, shared state managed through React context, Firebase handling auth, real-time sync, and file storage.

V3 React: Recruiter Step 1 — drag & drop resume zone at top (AI autofills name, email, phone from PDF), form fields below, availability paste field with AI parsing hint at bottom.
✓
New in the React build: Resume drag & drop with AI autofill. Team isolation by email domain. Conflict detection across the queue. Google Calendar integration with resume link in the event description. Interview name library configurable in Settings.
New in V3
The Resume Gate
March 2026 · First screen after login

After observing recruiters use the app, a pattern emerged: the first thing almost everyone did after logging in was scroll past all the form fields to find the resume upload. The form was the wrong first impression. The resume was the real starting point.

The fix: replace the form as the first screen with a full-screen drop zone. Drop a resume, Claude extracts name, email, and phone, and you land on Step 1 already filled in. Skip lands you on the same blank form. Either way — you choose how you want to work.

The Resume Gate — full-screen navy, personal greeting from Google auth, Resume / ATS Link toggle. Drop zone is the hero. Skip is the escape hatch.
ATS Link tab — disabled but wired. Headline and subtext update to match the active mode. Pre-configured for when an ATS API is connected.
✓
Why full-screen: The gate needs to feel like a moment, not a modal. Full navy background matches the login screen — it's a continuation of the auth flow, not the start of a form.
✓
Personal greeting: "Hey Ari." costs nothing and signals the app knows who you are. Sets the tone before any work happens.
→
ATS tab is pre-wired: Disabled today, but the UI and copy are already in place. When an ATS API is connected, it becomes functional without a redesign.
Retrospective

HONEST LOOK BACK.

Every version surfaced something. Here's what held up versus what I'd do differently starting today.

✓ What I'd keep
✓Starting with the spreadsheet. Forced clarity about the actual workflow before writing any UI code.
✓The persona split. Best architectural decision in the project. Every screen got simpler.
✓Vanilla JS first. Proved the concept cheaply before committing to a framework.
✓Coordinator-owned settings. Makes the tool usable by a real team without IT.
✓The ticket queue model. Right mental model for how coordinators think about work.
→ What I'd do differently
→Start with React. Vanilla JS became unmaintainable faster than expected.
→Add Firebase earlier. Data loss between sessions blocked real user testing.
→Test with a real coordinator at v1. The persona split insight would have come months earlier.
→Design auto-archive upfront. Tickets accumulated with no clear expiry path.
→Plan for Google Calendar API from day one. Internal-only conflict detection has limited value.

SEE IT IN ACTION

Locked In. is live and pilot-ready. Try the full recruiter → coordinator → confirmed flow.
